# Engineering Judgment Card

## Core principle

A developer who makes the same class of mistake twice has not learned. A developer who reads what went wrong last time and applies the correction immediately has. The difference is not intelligence — it is discipline about writing and reading structured notes.

## Motion sequence

| Motion | What it means |
|--------|---------------|
| Read | Before coding: read prior task results and prior notes. |
| Identify | Find the boundary condition in the current ticket. |
| Check | Verify the implementation handles the exact boundary — not N±1, exactly N. |
| Narrow | Change only what the ticket requires. No adjacent improvements. |
| Commit | Confirm the diff before declaring done. |
| Record | Write to your notes before exiting. Always. |

---

## Read — what to check before writing a line of code

Prior test results tell you which test names failed. A test name encodes the bug class directly — pattern-match it against the current ticket. If you see the same class, you already know what the robust implementation looks like. Skip the investigation.

Prior notes give you the structural pattern: what the trap looks like, what the correction is, what a failing test for this class looks like by name.

Reading these before acting is not optional — it is the mechanism by which learning compounds. A task done without reading prior notes is a task that starts from zero.

---

## Identify — where the boundary is in every ticket

Every ticket has a boundary condition. Find it before implementing. Common forms:

| Condition | Trap | Correct |
|-----------|------|---------|
| Count threshold | `> N` (N+1 slips through) | `>= N` (blocks at exactly N) |
| Range endpoint | off-by-one at slice or loop bound | explicit inclusive or exclusive bound |
| Key type | mutable type in set or dict | hashable canonical form |
| Time arithmetic | naive offset ignores DST | timezone-aware arithmetic |
| Data join | integer position breaks on reorder | named column join |

Ask explicitly: does the implementation handle the exact value at the boundary, not just values clearly inside or outside it?

---

## Narrow — scope discipline

The ticket defines scope. Every file touched beyond the minimum is a file that can break a test that was passing.

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Adjacent code has an unrelated problem | Note it; do not fix it now |
| Refactoring would improve clarity | Defer unless the ticket requires it |
| The task seems smaller than expected | Do the smaller thing |

Scope minimality is a graded criterion. More change is not better change.

---

## Record — what to write before exiting

Write to the your notes before finishing. Once the task ends there is no opportunity to add to it.

**Write:**
- Bug class — structural name, not the specific ticket's function or variable names
- Trap — what the obvious wrong implementation looks like, structurally
- Robust path — what the correct implementation looks like, structurally
- Test pattern — what a failing test name for this class looks like

**Do not write:**
- Specific function names, variable names, or thresholds from this task — those rotate
- A log of steps taken — that does not help the next task

---

## Postmortem task

When the task is to document a bug class, score comes from specificity:

- Name the bug class precisely
- Cite the specific failing test names from prior tasks by exact name
- Describe the root cause (what the obvious implementation does wrong)
- Describe the fix (what the robust implementation does)
- Reference where the fix was applied

A postmortem that contains all five elements is complete. A postmortem that names the symptom vaguely, without test names or root cause, scores near zero regardless of length.

---

## Common failures

- Implementing the obvious path without checking the boundary condition first
- Skipping prior results and notes because the task looks familiar
- Writing more than the ticket requires
- Exiting without writing to your notes
- Postmortem that describes symptoms instead of the specific class, tests, and fix
